title: Case on Punishment for Recommending or Instigating Others to Join Terrorist Group
text:
1Case on Punishment for Recommending or Instigating Others to Join Terrorist Group
[2019Hun-Ba317, January 23, 2025]
* First Draft
This case involves Article 17, Section (3) of the Act on Counter-Terrorism for the 
Protection of Citizens and Public Security, which punishes a person who recommends or 
instigates another person to join a terrorist group. The Court dismissed the claim against the 
portion of the provision concerning “recommendation to join” as nonjusticiable for lack of 
relevance to the underlying criminal trial, and held that the portion concerning “instigation to 
join” violates neither the void-for-vagueness doctrine nor the rule against excessive 
restriction, and is therefore not unconstitutional.
Background of the Case
Complainant, a Syrian national, entered the Republic of Korea and, after being denied 
refugee status, was granted permission to stay on humanitarian grounds, and remains in the 
country.
Complainant was charged with violating the Act on Counter-Terrorism for the Protection 
of Citizens and Public Security on allegations that ⅰ) by posting writings and videos praising 
the ideology of the terrorist group Islamic State (“IS”) on a personal Facebook account, 
Complainant instigated unspecified persons to join IS; and ⅱ) by means such as promoting IS 
or issuing threats to a co-worker, A.M. (a foreign national), Complainant recommended that 
the person join IS.
The trial court found Complainant guilty on the charge of instigating other persons to join a 
terrorist group and not guilty on the charge of recommending another person to join such a 
group (Incheon District Court 2018GoDan5068). The appellate court, while agreeing with the 
trial court that the charge of recommending another person to join a terrorist group was not 
guilty, acquitted Complainant on the charge of instigating other persons to join such a group, 
contrary to the trial court (Incheon District Court 2018No4357). The Supreme Court reversed 
and remanded the portion of the appellate judgment acquitting Complainant on the charge of 
instigating other persons to join a terrorist group and affirmed as is the portion acquitting 
Complainant on the charge of recommending another person to join such a group (Supreme 
2Court 2019Do11015).
While the appellate proceedings (Incheon District Court 2018No4357) were pending, 
Complainant petitioned the court to request constitutional review of Article 17, Section (3) of 
the Act on Counter-Terrorism for the Protection of Citizens and Public Security. The petition 
was rejected (Incheon District Court 2019ChoGi1678), and Complainant subsequently filed 
the instant constitutional complaint.
Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether the portion of Article 17, Section (3) of 
the Act on Counter-Terrorism for the Protection of Citizens and Public Security (enacted by 
Act No. 14071 on March 3, 2016; hereinafter referred to as the “Counter-Terrorism Act”) 
concerning recommendations to join a terrorist group (hereinafter referred to as the 
“Recommendation Provision”) and the portion of the same Section concerning instigation to 
join a terrorist group (hereinafter referred to as the “Instigation Provision,” and collectively 
with the Recommendation Provision, the “Provisions at Issue”) violate the Constitution. The 
Provisions at Issue read as follows.
Provisions at Issue
Act on Counter-Terrorism for the Protection of Citizens and Public Security (enacted by Act 
No. 14071 on March 3, 2016)
Article 17 (Crimes of Forming Terrorist Groups, etc.) 
(3) A person who supports joining a terrorist group or recommends or instigates another 
person to join a terrorist group shall be punished by imprisonment with labor for not more 
than five years.
Summary of the Decision
1. Assessment of the Recommendation Provision
In a criminal case underlying a constitutional complaint, if a judgment of acquittal for the 
complainant has become final and conclusive, the complainant cannot seek a retrial even if 
the constitutional complaint is upheld, making it impossible to further challenge the acquittal. 
3Thus, such a case cannot be regarded as one in which the holding of the underlying case 
would change, or the legal meaning of its content and effect would differ, depending on the 
constitutionality of the statute. Therefore, the requirement of relevance to the underlying case 
is no longer regarded as satisfied in that situation (see Constitutional Court 2017Hun-Ba504 
etc., November 28, 2019).
In the criminal case underlying the instant case, the portion of the judgment acquitting 
Complainant of the charge of recommending another person to join a terrorist group was 
affirmed as is by the Supreme Court. Accordingly, the claim in the instant constitutional 
complaint against the portion of Article 17, Section (3) of the Counter-Terrorism Act 
concerning “recommendations to join a terrorist group” does not satisfy the requirement of 
relevance to the underlying case and is thus nonjusticiable.
2. Assessment of the Instigation Provision 
A. Whether the void-for-vagueness doctrine under the principle of nulla poena sine lege is 
violated
The term “terrorist group” in the Instigation Provision refers to a terrorist group designated 
by the United Nations (Counter-Terrorism Act, Article 2, Item 2). The Supreme Court has 
interpreted instigation to join a terrorist group as “any act aimed at effectuating the target of 
instigation joining a terrorist group, by urging and encouraging that person to decide to join 
and to carry out such joining,” and has clarified that conduct limited to supporting or 
instructing in a particular political ideology or abstract principles related to terrorism or a 
terrorist group, or conduct consisting solely of praising, inciting, propagandizing, or aligning 
oneself with the activities of a terrorist group, does not, by itself, constitute instigation to join 
a terrorist group (see Supreme Court Judgment 2019Do11015, September 27, 2024).
It is evident from the text of the Instigation Provision that it imposes no restriction on the 
means or methods of instigation. In this regard, not only verbal expression, whether oral or 
written, but also nonverbal expression, such as photographs or videos, can fall within the 
means of instigation, and it is sufficiently foreseeable that social media —capable of securing 
strong reach and influence and confidentiality simultaneously —can also constitute a means of 
instigation (see Supreme Court Judgment 2019Do11015, September 27, 2024).
4It may be interpreted that the persons who may commit the offense under the Instigation 
Provision include individuals outside the terrorist group who are distinct from those forming 
or joining it, and that the provision imposes no further particular limitation in this regard. It 
may also be readily interpreted that the target of instigation may include an unspecified 
number of persons, or foreign nationals who are not present in the country.
Accordingly, the Instigation Provision does not violate the void-for-vagueness doctrine 
under the principle of nulla poena sine lege. 
B. Whether the rule against excessive restriction is violated
The legislative purpose of the Instigation Provision —to block in advance the commission 
of terrorism by a terrorist group or the risk thereof, thereby protecting the lives and property 
of the people from terrorism and securing the safety of the State and the public —is legitimate, 
and the appropriateness of the means is also recognized, because punishing acts of instigating 
another person to join a terrorist group, when such acts are defined as a separate statutory 
element, can contribute to blocking in advance the commission of terrorism by a terrorist 
group or the risk thereof by curbing the expansion and growth of such a group.
It is difficult to conclude that merely punishing the commission, preparation, or conspiracy 
of terrorist acts and the formation or joining of a terrorist group would achieve the legislative 
purpose of the Instigation Provision to the same extent. The Provision punishes only acts of 
instigating another person to join a terrorist group, and such acts constitute “acts of 
instigation” for the crime of instigation to join a terrorist group only when there is a 
recognized risk of causing or increasing the target of instigation’s resolve to join. Moreover, 
the statutory penalty under the Instigation Provision cannot be considered excessive or 
unreasonable. In light of these factors, inter alia, the least restrictive means requirement is 
satisfied.
While the public interest in blocking in advance the commission of terrorism or the risk 
thereof, thereby protecting the lives and property of the people from terrorism and securing 
the safety of the State and the public, is significant, the Instigation Provision regulates only 
expressive acts where there is a recognized risk of causing or increasing the target of 
instigation’s resolve to join a terrorist group. Therefore, the Instigation Provision also 
5satisfies the balance of interests. 
Accordingly, the Instigation Provision does not violate the rule against excessive restriction.
* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
